body.u: I am 68 years old and am sitting in my chair looking back over my life. I Remember leaving school in May 1978 and at the time I played in a Rock band, I always thought we would make it high with that band. I got myself a four year apprenticshipapprenticeship as an electrical fitter but I never used it apart from around the home and doing odd jobs for friends. I Remember passing my driving test at 17, that was the age you could drive then, and really looking forward to my first driving lesson, after all I waited since I was about 6 years old for that. The only think is though, I could never afford a car till I was about 21. That’s when I got my HGV licence, dead chuffed about that I was, I would bring my 35 ton lorry up our small culdisackcul-de-sac and feel I were king. When I had settled down in the lorry business I married, this must have been about 1986-7 perioudperiod. We stayed with my mother for a coupldcouple of years and eventullyeventually moved into a flat nearby. That period was pretty tough for me. I was used to going out with my mates spending all my money and genrallygenerally having a laugh. But all of a sudden I was faced with rent problems. Gas electricity etc and found it difficult to adapt but I soon as my wife started work things got a bit better. In August 1989 we had our first child, a boy. that was probably the happiest moment of my life, I couldn’t get over that there was another ‘West’ in the world and I was responsible. I was always proud of the west’s and was heartbroken when I found I couldn’t keep the name in ‘Geordie Land’ where it origonnalyoriginally came from. My wife had to stop work through the child and things started to look bad again, it seemed that every penny I was bringing in was going to bills, food and clothing. I had a lot of pleasure watching my child grow up, and was always boasting about him, “Oh my little one is walking now!” I would tell everyone. I was the proudest man in the world when it came to him. When he grew up and started school my wife started work again and we put a little money away each week when we deciededdecided to get right out of our worry’s and surroundings and I booked the three of us for a fortnight in Spain. That was really great, sunbathing and just getting away from all those troubles back home.

When we returned, I was sitting by the fire thinking of my schooldays, all the laughs we had, and I thought about the group I played in, really made me laugh that did, and then I got in touch with those long lost friends and we just had to get out the Guitars and drums etc, and I always remember thinking, “blimyblimey we were rubbish” as the years went past and I got older I seemed to be drifting away from those happy years, my son was 16 by this time and I was nearly an old man. One thing I remember is when I was about 55 I was talking the dog out for a walk and saw all the kids playing football on the pitch I used to play on, and one of them said {‘whan ‘a’ Game mr P”} the joy of that was unbelievable and I went into hastericshysterics of laughter and in the end I played. It looked quite odd, this 55 year old man playing football with a bunch of school kids, mind you. I enjoyed every minute of it. And when I had to retire from thr Lorry’s which I was so attached to that really broke my heart. I got awarded with a smart watch though and on my father’s advice I saved from the age of 30 for my retirement as he had passed on I couldn’t thank him for his advice but I sure did appricateappreciate it. I brought my wife and I an old cottage up in the Newcastle area which I worshipped since I was a young lad. The cottage was lovely and cozeycosy with a chair at the window where I once sat down and rethoughtre-thought my whole life. That was about 2 hours ago.
